coredata.dc:title: Engineering of ethylenediamine-functionalized 0D carbon dots for optical bi-mode determination of Hg(II), fluorescent sand, and logic gate operations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chacko, Anu Rose
Mathew, Sneha
Thara, Chinnu R.
John, Neenamol
Mathew, Jincy
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive photoluminescence turn-off Hg(II) sensor based on ethylenediamine-functionalized carbon dots (CDs/en) fabricated using wastepaper and ethylenediamine via a facile two-step synthesis process. A quantum yield (QY) obtained is 16.2 % with maximum emission at 446 nm. The CDs/en acted as the blue fluorescent probe accompanied by strong quenching of the fluorescence emission with Hg(II) at pH 6. The amino or oxygen groups on the CDs/en allow electron transfer to Hg(II), leading to fluorescence quenching. Under the optimal condition, the proposed sensor was applied to detect Hg(II) in the linear range of 1–20 nM. A detection limit of 0.16 nM is achieved using fluorescence spectroscopy and 0.72 nM by monitoring the absorbance variation at 274 nm. The successful real sample analysis and “EXIT” signs made of fluorescent sand also paved the way for the best optical sensor. Based on the bi-mode signals, a “NAND” logic gate was constructed effectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ethylenediamine
Carbon dots
Bi-mode sensing
electron transfer
Fluorescent sand
“EXIT” sign
Logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bi-mode sensing of Hg(II) using CDs/en
Comparison of CDs/en and undoped CDs in PET mechanism
Formulation and performance of fluorescent sand

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India for providing University Junior Research Fellowship and DST SAIF for providing the instrumentation facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Variation in the PL intensity of CDs synthesized under different temperatures and time, and (b) precursor ratio of CDs and EN.
(a) Absorption, (b) PL, (c) excitation-emission spectra, (d) HRTEM image, (e) size distribution, and (f) AFM image of CDs/en. The color scale is in arbitrary units.
(a) XPS full spectra, (b) C 1s XPS spectra, (c) O 1s XPS spectra of CDs, (d) C 1s XPS spectra, (e) N 1s XPS spectra, and (f) Fourier transform infrared spectra of CDs/en.
(a) Fluorescence intensity at 446 nm, (b) absorbance at 274 nm, and (c) the photograph of the CDs/en in the absence (blank) and presence of 20 nM different metal ions.

(a) PL spectra of the CDs/en in the presence of different Hg(II) concentrations (1–20 nM), (b) relationship between the PL intensity of CDs/en and the concentration of Hg(II) at 1–7 and 16–20 nM, (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of CDs/en in the presence of various concentrations of Hg(II) solution, and (d) UV–vis. spectrum of CDs/en with the concentrations of Hg(II) at 1–20 nM. Inset: linearity of response.
(a) Stern-Volmer plot based on emission data, (b) Job's plot based on absorption data, and (c) Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) plot based on absorption data.

(a) Absorption spectrum of Hg(II) (20 nM) and excitation and emission spectra of CDs/en, (b) time-resolved fluorescence decays of CDs/en (black), CDs/en + Hg(II) mixture (red) upon emission at 446 nm, and (c) schematic illustration of the fluorescence sensor based on the CDs/en selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II).

(a) PL intensity of CDs and CD/en. Left inset: Photograph of fluorescence of CDs and CD/en, right inset: QY of CDs and CD/en, (b) PL lifetime of CDs and CD/en, (c) Tauc plot of CDs and CD/en, and (d) PL quenching mechanism profile of CD/en towards Hg(II).
Fluorescent sand-based exit signs under (a) daylight, and (b) UV light.
(a) Logic scheme, (b) truth table, (c) fluorescence intensity, and (d) absorbance under 2 different input conditions. The concentration of CDs/en and Hg(II) were 10 and 20 nM, respectively.
Illustration of the synthesis of CDs/en and their application as a bi-mode optical probe for the determination of Hg(II).
Comparison of sensors based on CDs for the detection of Hg(II).

head.ce:title: Engineering of ethylenediamine-functionalized 0D carbon dots for optical bi-mode determination of Hg(II), fluorescent sand, and logic gate operations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills PO, Kottayam, Kerala 686560, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills PO, Kottayam, Kerala, INDIA 686560

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive photoluminescence turn-off Hg(II) sensor based on ethylenediamine-functionalized carbon dots (CDs/en) fabricated using wastepaper and ethylenediamine via a facile two-step synthesis process. A quantum yield (QY) obtained is 16.2 % with maximum emission at 446 nm. The CDs/en acted as the blue fluorescent probe accompanied by strong quenching of the fluorescence emission with Hg(II) at pH 6. The amino or oxygen groups on the CDs/en allow electron transfer to Hg(II), leading to fluorescence quenching. Under the optimal condition, the proposed sensor was applied to detect Hg(II) in the linear range of 1–20 nM. A detection limit of 0.16 nM is achieved using fluorescence spectroscopy and 0.72 nM by monitoring the absorbance variation at 274 nm. The successful real sample analysis and “EXIT” signs made of fluorescent sand also paved the way for the best optical sensor. Based on the bi-mode signals, a “NAND” logic gate was constructed effectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past several decades, the concentration of heavy metals in the environment has increased due to rapid industrialization and other anthropogenic activities. Among them, mercury (Hg(II)) is one of the top ten chemicals or chemical groups that the WHO considers the most significant public health concern. Mercury can contaminate water and spread through the food chain, ultimately disrupting human body functions. This can lead to kidney failure, nervous system damage, and severe heart complications [1]. Currently, the mobility of mercury in the food chain is mainly associated with drinking water. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) issued the maximum contaminant level of Hg (II) ions in drinking water is 2 μg/l. [2] Recently, the exalted amount of mercury in water and water bodies has become one of the issues of concern. The quantitative toxicity assessment due to mercury in water became an agreed attention. Therefore, there is a necessitate to develop an efficient, economical, and fast detection technique for mercury [3].

Several technologies such as cold-vapor atomical fluorescence and absorption spectrometry [4], chemical precipitation [5], ion exchange [6], inductance-connected plasma emission spectrometry [7], solvent extraction [8], mass spectrometry [9], amalgamation [10], coagulation [11], colorimetric [12], membrane filtration [13], adsorption [14], and electrochemical detection [15] have been widely employed for the quantitative determination of mercury in water. However, most of these methods are confined by highly costly operations and/or may also be ineffectual for the prompt detection of mercury ions, mainly at trace-level concentrations. Moreover, preprocessing techniques in the mentioned methods, such as centrifugation, precipitation, and filtration, are not only usually resource-intensive and expensive but can also lead to malformations, such as deformation and inactivation, especially in the investigation of an exemplary real sample [16]. However, despite the apparent pros and cons of the distant and extremely virulent characteristics of mercury, simple fluorescence-based sensing techniques for the detection of mercury are appealing and have advantages such as low cost, simple instrumentation, and high sensitivity [17]. These remarkable advantages make fluorescence monitoring a dependable and strong method for routine analytical detection [18]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention as zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial, and have emerged as promising candidates in various fields due to their unique optical, electronic, chemical, and biocompatible properties [19]. CDs exhibit excellent photoluminescence, tunable fluorescence, aqueous solubility, and low toxicity, making them versatile in a wide array of applications. Their surface functionalization capabilities further enhance their relevance, allowing the tailoring of properties to meet specific needs. CDs can be synthesized from inexpensive and abundant carbon sources, contributing to their sustainability and affordability, which sets them apart from conventional semiconductor-based quantum dots. This introduction aims to provide a deep dive into potential applications across multiple domains, including bioimaging [20], drug delivery [21], sensors [22,23], energy conversion [24], environmental remediation [25], photosensitizer [26], photocatalysis [27], light-emitting diodes [28,29,30], anti-counterfeiting [31,32], and various biological fields [33,34]. However, CDs still have specific limitations, such as short fluorescence lifetime, low fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and poor selectivity concerning detection. Functionalization, particularly through heteroatom doping and surface modification, is a key method for adjusting the physical and chemical properties of CDs [35,36]. Doping with a heteroatom is an efficient and convenient method that increases the number of active sites and functional groups on the surface of CDs, expanding their potential applications. Moreover, nonmetal element doping (such as N, P, S, and B), is more well-liked and ecologically amiable than metal doping. N-doped CDs (N-CDs) are useful in several areas, including bioimaging, catalysis, and sensing [37–40].
Details of materials and methods, synthesis, real sample analysis, and characterizations are added in supporting information (SI, Experimental details).

The CDs/en solution was placed in a 10 mm optical path length quartz fluorescence cuvette and analyzed using a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer. The PL spectrum of 3 ml CD/en was recorded consequently, and the PL intensity was set as F0. The Hg(II) ions were detected by PL quenching. Various concentrations of Hg(II) ions ranging between 1 and 20 nM were added into the CD/en solution and were diluted in 0.1 M PBS at pH 6. The mixed solutions were kept stationary at room temperature for 1 min, and the PL quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm were finally recorded. For optimizing the test condition, the effect of pH, temperature and ionic strength on PL intensity of the CD/en was investigated. Some other standard metal ion stock solutions (Ag+, Al3+, Bi3+, Cd2+, Co3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Zr4+) were selected to detect the selectivity of the CDs/en. The PL data were collected for each experimental condition, and the results were displayed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD).

The ‘fluorescent sand’ was manufactured using CDs/en as a fluorescent solution and silica gel granules. Mix the prepared fluorescent solution with silica gel. The amount of CD/en solution used will depend on the preferred fluorescence intensity and the absorption capacity of the silica gel. Stirred the mixture thoroughly to ensure uniform distribution of the fluorescent solution over the silica gel matrix. Allow the mixture to air dry. After drying, the ‘fluorescent sand’ was ready to test the fluorescence under a suitable wavelength. Stored the prepared product in a dry and airtight container.

This type of system responds to simple single-output logic gate operations. We have demonstrated the NAND logic properties of CDs/en with Hg(II). In the NAND logic gate operations, 10 nM CD/en and 20 nM Hg(II) were used as the two inputs to activate the sensing platform. Other procedures were the same as that for Hg(II) detection in the sensing system.

Herein, we employed a simple and low-cost method to fabricate fluorescent CD/en using a top-down approach assisted by hydrothermal reaction. To optimize the synthesis of fluorescent CD/en, we systematically adjusted key reaction parameters, including temperature, precursor molar ratio, and reaction period. It was observed that extending the reaction time to 10 h at 180 °C further enhanced the PL intensity (Fig. 1a). Additionally, both the PL intensity and quantum yield increased with the rise in temperature to 180 °C. Therefore, the optimal temperature and reaction period for synthesizing CD were determined to be 180 °C for 10 h. The amount and composition of the raw materials play a crucial role in synthesizing CD/en. Functionalization of CDs with ethylenediamine was achieved through ultrasonication for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 1b, the optimization of the CD/en ratio was varied from 2/1 to 12/1 while keeping the volume of the solvent (100 ml) constant. Notably, the highest PL intensity was achieved at a ratio of 4/1 (3 g CDs and 0.75 ml EN), indicating the need for a higher carbon dots concentration than ethylenediamine. This optimization ensured the production of highly fluorescent CD/en with excellent stability and dispersibility.

To explore the enthralling optical properties of the CD/en, the UV–vis. absorption and PL emission and excitation spectra were obtained. The absorption spectra of the CD/en solution show a sharp terminal peak centered at 205 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds [54]. Another peak at 274 nm corresponds to the n-π* transitions of CO in CD/en (Fig. 2a) [55]. The CD/en solution appears transparent brown under sunlight and displays a noticeable deep-blue color when exposure to UV light (inset in Fig. 2b) at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of CD/en was estimated to be 16.2 % for quinine sulfate as a standard. The emission peak is centered at about 446 nm with a (full-width at half-maximum) FWHM of 89 nm, and shows relatively broad luminescence in the deep-blue region. The CIE standard chromaticity coordinates of deep-blue emission are (0.17, 0.19) (Fig. 2b). The emission peak of CD/en exhibits excitation-independent characteristics and the wavelength of the maximum emission shows no substantial changes under the excitation from 300 to 360 nm (Figs. 2c, S1a). The PL excitation spectra of CD/en show a peak at 350 nm (Fig. S1b).

For applications, as we know, fluorescent nanomaterials are beneficial if they have high stability. The photostability of CD/en was demonstrated through continuous UV light (365 nm) irradiation. The PL intensity showed no significant decrease after 60 min of exposure (Fig. S1c), indicating their excellent photostability. We conducted burst analysis measurements to monitor the change in fluorescence intensity at the single-molecule level. The results are displayed in Fig. S1d. The CD/en probe concentration was kept at 20 nM, revealing that the intensity burst is consistent across all trials, with minimal variation. The emission intensity and spectra show no obvious change after being stored for six months at room temperature (Fig. S1e). Furthermore, the PL intensity of CD/en decreased at higher temperatures, and the PL was restored when the temperature returned to 30 °C. The results demonstrated a strong linear correlation between the PL intensity and the temperature within the range of 30–70 °C. In other words, the temperature cannot cause permanent destruction on the surface structure of the CD/en in the same temperature range (Fig. S1f). The effect of pH on the PL intensity was also evaluated from pH 4 to 9. As seen in Fig. S1g, The PL intensity was altered in alkaline or acidic conditions except unchanged under neutral conditions. The pH had an impact on the PL intensity of CD/en, which differed from most of the others noted in the reports [56,57]. We also have investigated the effect of ionic strength on the PL intensity of the CD/en. As seen in Fig. S1h, different concentrations of NaCl solutions ranging from 0 to 1 M have remained almost unchanged in PL intensity. The morphology and diameter of the CD/en were revealed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2d shows that the obtained CD/en is a uniform dispersion of nanoparticles with an average size of 7.82 ± 0.50 nm (Fig. 2e). Fig. 2f shows the AFM image of CDs/en. From the image, it is evident that the particles are quasi-spherical, with an average height of 3.65 nm. The dispersion is good, and there are no apparent signs of agglomeration. During the preparation of AFM test samples, CDs/en were directly mixed in deionized water, which indicated that the as-prepared CDs/en had a good dispersion in water.

The chemical components and functional groups on the surfaces of CDs/en were further explored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. The full XPS spectra show that, following EN functionalization, the nitrogen content increased from barely observable above the baseline to 12.3 %. There are three obvious peaks at 284.7, 400.0, and 531.9 eV, which are assigned to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 3a). According to the C 1 s spectra of CD (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 284.7, 285.9, and 288.8 eV confirmed the presence of CC, C-O-C, and CO, respectively. The O 1 s spectra of CD (Fig. 3c) exhibit one dominant peak at 531.9 eV, which contributes to C-OH/C-O-C bonds [58]. The signals of the oxygen-containing functional group of CO (285.9 eV) in high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of CDs disappeared in CDs/en. On the other hand, the nitrogen-containing functional group of CN (286.0 eV) was observed in the C 1 s spectra of CDs/en after EN functionalization (Fig. 3d). The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of CDs/en (Fig. 3e) also demonstrate that the nitrogens are mainly in the form of amino groups such as CN (400.0 eV) and NH (401.5 eV). The high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. S2a) revealed two distinct peaks centered at 531.2 and 532.2 eV, which are attributed to CO and C-OH/C-O-C. From the FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 3f, the stretching vibration bands of CO (1240 cm−1), OH, and COOH (broadband at 3230 cm−1) decreased after EN functionalization, while the signals of the stretching vibration bands of NH (3250 cm−1) and CN (1433 cm−1) increased in CDs/en compared with that of CDs. Consistent with XPS results and FT-IR analysis, once again confirm that abundant nitrogen and oxygen functional groups are imported on the surface of CDs/en. The higher degree of graphitization of the CDs/en is reflected in their Raman spectra (Fig. S2b). It presented two typical peaks at ~1340 and ~ 1562 cm−1, which are ascribed to the D and G bands [59]. The XRD pattern of CDs/en (Fig. S2c) showed a peak centered at 22.1°, indicating the graphite-like structures [60]. Furthermore, the zeta-potential (Fig. S2d) of the CDs/en was −14.8 mV, indicating a few negatively charged groups, such as -COOH and -OH, on their surface [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bi-mode sensing of Hg(II) using CDs/en
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and specificity of composed CDs/en are crucial issues for exploring their potential applications in sensors and comparative experiment studies. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , the PL intensity, absorbance, and the photograph under UV light of CDs/en were recorded by adding 14 common metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Bi3+, Cd2+, Co3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Zr4+) at the concentration of 20 nM under the same conditions. It was obvious that Hg(II) could incite strong PL quenching, while other metal ions showed almost no quenching (Fig. 4a). Similar to PL quenching, significant ultraviolet absorption was observed at 274 nm (Fig. 4b). The CDs/en and different metal ions exhibit strong blue fluorescence under irradiation from a 350 nm lamp. However, the fluorescence change was observed only upon adding Hg(II) (Fig. 4c). Our findings indicate a unique PL intensity and fluorescence change with Hg(II) that was not replicated by the other tested heavy metals. These features indicate that Hg(II) has a stronger affinity for nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the surface of CDs/en than other metal ions. Therefore, the CDs/en-based fluorescence and UV sensing platform was made successfully to sensitively and selectively detect Hg(II). Some experimental parameters, such as the reaction time and pH of CDs/en, were first optimized to achieve high sensitivity. The response time for Hg(II) detection is very short, and the reaction can be performed within a few seconds (Fig. S3a). An optimal reaction time of 1 min was selected in order to produce more precise results. To acquire the preferred detection performance, a detailed investigation of the effect of pH on Hg(II) sensing was conducted. As illustrated in Fig. S3b, the pH varies from 4.0 to 9.0, the maximum ΔF (ΔF is the variation in fluorescence intensity with and without Hg(II)), and ΔA (ΔA is the variation in absorbance with and without Hg(II)) were found at pH = 6.0. At lower pH values, functional groups like amines, which may compete with protons in acidic environments, reduce binding affinity to Hg(II). Conversely, at higher pH levels, OH− ion can interact with Hg(II), leading to the formation of insoluble Hg(OH)+ or Hg(OH)2 complexes, which hinder the effective quenching. Optimal fluorescence quenching was observed at pH 6, where the binding affinity between Hg(II) and the functional groups on CD/en was maximized. Other pH environments disrupted the electron transfer efficiency and fluorescence response. Thus, maintaining the pH within an optimal range is essential for achieving reliable and sensitive detection of Hg(II) using CD/en. Hence, pH = 6 was selected in the ensuing experiments.

From Fig. 5a, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs/en decreases linearly when the concentration of Hg(II) increases from 1 to 20 nM. The relationship between Hg(II) concentration and the fluorescent signal displayed a good linear correlation in the range 1–7 to 16–20 nM (Fig. 5b). The linear equation is PL intensity = (−1100.60 ± 15.71) [Hg(II)] nM + (23,937.68 ± 283.53) (R2 = 0.998), where the limit of detection (LOD) for Hg(II), calculated following the signal/noise ratio of 3, to be 0.16 nM. The color of CDs/en under different Hg(II) concentrations was further confirmed with the CIE diagram, which is accordant with the PL spectrum displayed in Fig. 5a. The CIE titration curve unveils that the PL color changes from blue to yellowish white (Fig. 5c). Also, the absorbance showed a good linear relationship in the range 1–20 nM with a detection limit of 0.72 nM following the 3σ International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) criteria, where the regression equation was y = (−0.097) x + (2.174) with R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 5d). In drinking water, the safe limit of Hg(II) was set as below 2 ppb (9.6 nM) by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA), the World Health Organization (WHO), and the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS).

To understand the limit of fluorescence quenching with Hg(II) ions, the quenching constant (K
sv ) was calculated to be 1.46 × 108 M−1 on the basis of Stern-Volmer equation F
0 /F = 1 + K
sv [Q] (Fig. 6a) [62]. Where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs/en in the absence and presence of Hg(II), respectively, [Q] is the concentration of Hg(II). Further, the binding stoichiometry between the CDs/en and Hg(II) ions was investigated while employing the methods of Job's plot of continuous variation. The graph was plotted between absorbance and mole fraction of CDs/en as depicted in Fig. 6b. This analysis revealed a 1:1 stoichiometry for a probe-Hg(II) interaction [63]. In addition, the binding constant (K a) depending on absorption spectral data between CDs/en and Hg(II) was calculated to be 7.4 × 107 M−1 on the basis of the Benesi-Hildebrand (BH) method. This is displayed in Fig. 6c, which reflects the strength of the interaction between the probe and Hg(II) [64]. To examine the selectivity of a PL probe during the determination of Hg(II), some metal ions in the reaction medium with concentrations of 40 nM and 20 nM of Hg(II) were carefully examined under identical conditions. As described in Fig. S4, it was observed that other species show almost no significant interference on the F/F0 values. CDs/en exhibit exceptional selectivity towards Hg(II) in comparison to other metal ions. Therefore, proving the potential of the CDs/en-based sensor for the sensitive and selective determination of Hg(II) via bi-mode sensing can be successfully applied.

Hg(II) can damage the nervous system, kidneys, liver, and immune system. Therefore, it is important to accurately detect Hg(II) in real samples. Water samples from various sources were selected as the real samples to assess the practical performance of the sensor. Both PL intensities and UV–vis. signals were recorded. As illustrated in Table S1, the recovery was in the range of 99.70–107.25 % with the RSD ≤ 0.16 %. These results clearly illustrated the practicality of using the CDs/en-based sensor for the determination of Hg(II) in different water sources.

The possible sensing mechanism of a CDs/en-based PL sensor was studied through PL, UV–vis. absorption and lifetime measurements. As shown in Fig. 7a, the PL excitation or emission spectrum of CDs/en clearly does not overlap with the absorbance spectrum of Hg(II). Therefore, the possibility of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between CDs/en and Hg(II) is eliminated. In order to further confirm the quenching mechanism between CDs/en and Hg(II), we measured the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the CDs/en in the absence and presence of Hg(II) (Fig. 7b). The fluorescence decay kinetics of CDs/en and CDs/en + Hg(II) were analyzed using a classical three-exponential decay function. The lifetime of CDs/en was 4.31 ns, while the lifetime of CDs/en + Hg(II) decreased to 3.35 ns. The shortened lifetime of the system resulted in the elimination of the inner filter effect (IFE) and indicated that the quenching is a dynamic quenching mechanism. Also, the difference in the lifetime analysis of the two systems can be attributed to the significant photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between Hg(II) ions and CDs/en, resulting in non-radiative recombination and annihilation of electrons and holes. This finding suggests the possibility of a PET mechanism [65]. The recognition process is illustrated in Fig. 7c. CD/en can act as an electron donor, and Hg(II) acts as an electron acceptor in the PET process. The mechanism of PET with CD/en and Hg(II) is given below:

Upon excitation, an electron from the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) of the CD/en is promoted to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO). Ethylenediamine introduces amine groups, which shift the energy levels, typically lowering the band gap and making excitation more efficient.
CD / en + hν → CD / en
excited state
∗

After excitation, the CD/en has an electron in its LUMO that is available for transfer. Hg(II) ions are strong electron acceptors due to their high electron affinity. When the excited electron from the CD/en transfers to Hg(II), it can reduce Hg(II) to Hg(I) or form a transient coordination complex.
CD / en
excited state
∗
+ Hg
II
→ CD / en
excited state
+
+ Hg
I
∗

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CDs/en and undoped CDs in PET mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen doping plays a crucial role in enhancing the PET efficiency in carbon dots. This modification improves both the electronic structure and surface chemistry of the CDs, significantly boosting their electron-donating capabilities [66]. Our studies revealed that N-doped and undoped CDs have shown that the doped sample exhibited improved fluorescence, and PL intensity, with a quantum yield of 16.2 % compared to 4.1 % for the undoped CDs, as shown in Fig. 8a. Furthermore, CDs/en exhibit improved fluorescence lifetimes compared to undoped CDs, primarily due to enhanced charge separation and reduced non-radiative recombination (Fig. 8b). Tauc plots were obtained from the absorption spectra of the CDs and CDs/en show direct energy gaps corresponding to the n → π* transitions of 3.4 eV for the CDs and 3.0 eV for the CDs/en (Fig. 8c). The functional groups within the CDs lattice acted as electron donors, contributing to an increase in the Fermi energy of the CDs. Additionally, the CD/en exhibited a lower bandgap value, indicating a higher density of energy states due to the doping process. This makes it easier for CD/en to donate electrons, facilitating efficient electron transfer under light irradiation. This enhanced PET mechanism is particularly evident when interacting with Hg(II). Being in an electron-deficient state, its LUMO lies between the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the CD/en, meaning that the electron is transferred from the CD/en to the Hg(II) easier than the CDs. In other words, after the CD/en is excited, an electron from its HOMO is transferred to its LUMO, and then immediately passes through a non-radiative transition into the vacant LUMO of the Hg(II), leading to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 8d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation and performance of fluorescent sand
ce:section.ce:para: Building on the above results and noteworthy characteristics of CDs/en, we extended the probability of the PL probe to the next level. Outstanding fluorescence factors of CDs/en were used to make fluorescent sand. Fluorescent sand is typically treated or coated with materials that exhibit fluorescence under exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light. To ensure safety and quality, we have chosen silica gel as a base material for fluorescent sand. The silica gel should be clean and free from impurities for optimal performance. Evenly distribute the synthesized fluorescent material CDs/en with silica gel that absorbs UV light and emits visible light at a longer wavelength. The performance, stability, and durability of fluorescent sand were often measured by its fluorescence intensity. This material is safe for handling and does not pose any environmental hazards. The intended applications of fluorescent sand are for decorative purposes, educational tools, artistic projects, and industrial applications such as inks or coatings.

As seen in many stores today, we can use fluorescent sand for safe “EXIT” signs. Exit signs are crucial for guiding people to safety in emergencies. There are various types of exit signs available, but the ones that require particular disposal are those that contain tritium, a radioactive gas. The US Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) considers tritium exit signs as solid waste, which requires specific licenses for their disposal [67]. Instead of such high-risk materials, we can use fluorescent sand made of CDs/en and silica gel (Fig. 9 ). The developed fluorescent sand-based exit signs are readily visible in normal and emergency lighting conditions and are designed with high contrast for easy visibility from a distance. Furthermore, fluorescent sand has many benefits, including cost-effectiveness, non-radioactivity, non-toxicity, and flame retardancy. As a result, it is an excellent alternative to traditional exit signs.

Based on the above discussion, a “NAND” logic gate was successfully devised. The logic scheme and truth table of the proposed “NAND” gate are shown in Fig. 10a & b. As depicted in Fig. 10b, the two inputs were CDs/en (input 1) and Hg(II) (input 2), where the presence of CDs/en and Hg(II) was set as “1” and their absence as “0”. The two outputs were defined as fluorescence emission at 446 nm and ultraviolet absorption at 274 nm. For these outputs, a value of 1 was assigned to normal fluorescence intensity and apparent ultraviolet absorption, while the “off” state (0) corresponded to quenched fluorescence and low UV absorption. The outputs for different input combinations are depicted in Fig. 10c & d. In the absence of any input (0,0), the output is 1 when it leads to only a CD signal. In the presence of either input 1 (1,0) or input 2 (0,1), the CDs + CDs/en and CDs + Hg(II) complexes are formed their signals also yielded an output of 1. When both inputs (1,1) were added, both fluorescence and absorbance signals of CDs + CDs/en were quenched by Hg(II), resulting in an output of 0.

In summary, a low-cost and facile two-step hydrothermal method for the preparation of CDs/en was constructed using wastepaper and ethylenediamine as precursors in this work. Fluorescence and absorbance measurements were performed to detect Hg(II) from 1 to 20 nM using CDs/en. Furthermore, this assay has distinct advantages. The proposed strategy includes two signals that provide sufficient credibility. The synthesis and detection processes are simple, convenient, and environmental friendly, requiring no harmful solvents. The presented assay of bi-mode signal output can be used for detection in some real samples. Also, the concept of using fluorescent sand of CDs/en and silica gel for exit signs was intriguing, and it's crucial to balance creativity with practicality and safety. With significant fluorescence intensity and absorbance induced by the input of CDs/en and Hg(II), a “NAND” logic gate was constructed successfully. This work could offer valuable insights for the sequential detection of Hg(II) ions.

Anu Rose Chacko: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Sneha Mathew: Data curation. Chinnu R. Thara: Data curation. Neenamol John: Data curation. Jincy Mathew: Data curation. Beena Mathew: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India for providing University Junior Research Fellowship and DST SAIF for providing the instrumentation facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111806.
